relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: David's force returns to find its refugee town burned.
Women and children have been abducted rather than killed, leaving terror and uncertainty.
The displaced fighters confront the destruction of home and capture of family.
They weep together until their bodies cannot continue.
Ahinoam and Abigail are named among the captives rather than reduced to generic spoil.
Grief turns toward threatened internal violence against David, who seeks strength in YHWH.
David asks Abiathar to bring the ephod, drawing on the priest who survived Saul's massacre.
The answer directs pursuit toward rescue.
Six hundred begin the chase and reach the Besor crossing.
Two hundred exhausted people remain behind; incapacity is not desertion.
They find an Egyptian worker alone in the field and first provide food and water.
After substantial nourishment, his life returns from three days of abandonment.
He identifies himself as an enslaved/servile Egyptian abandoned by an Amalekite master when sick.
He reports the raids, including the burning of Ziklag.
Before guiding David, he negotiates protection from death and return to the abusive master.
He leads them to a celebrating raiding camp spread across the land.
David's force attacks for more than a day; four hundred mounted young men escape.
The captives and taken goods are recovered, including David's wives.
The narrator emphasizes full restoration of people and property.
Additional animals are claimed and labeled David's spoil, keeping the economics of war visible.
David returns to the exhausted two hundred and greets them in peace.
Some fighters attempt to exclude the noncombatants from goods while returning only their families.
David rejects the exclusion and attributes survival and victory to gift rather than individual entitlement.
He establishes equal shares for frontline fighters and those guarding the communal base.
The equity rule becomes enduring policy.
David distributes captured goods among Judah's elders and allied communities.
The list begins with Bethel, Ramoth-negev, and Jattir.
It continues through Aroer, Siphmoth, and Eshtemoa.
Jerahmeelite and Kenite towns are included.
Hormah, Bor-ashan, and Athach receive portions.
Hebron and the places that hosted David's roaming community are remembered.

contemplative_note: The abandoned worker becomes the guide to restoration only after he is fed and allowed to negotiate safety. Besor teaches that exhausted people remain full members of community and that unseen support labor deserves an equal share.
